skill.name: multi-agent-patterns
skill.description: Guides the design of multi-agent systems using supervisor, swarm, hierarchical, handoff, consensus, and failure-handling patterns.
skill.summary: Design practical multi-agent workflows with context isolation, coordination protocols, and failure controls.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown diagrams, instructional code snippets, and ordinary words such as agent keys. No prompt injection, credential access, command execution, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found. A low-risk warning remains because documentation includes copyable filesystem coordination examples that need validation before production use.

low_findings.title: Copyable Filesystem Coordination Examples Need Hardening
low_findings.description: The framework reference includes example shared-state and checkpoint code that builds file paths from caller-provided identifiers. This is documentation, not active skill behavior, but users should add path validation and locking before production use. Verdict: true positive as a low-risk documentation concern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write examples are explicit and easy to locate. Risk is limited because they are Markdown examples rather than code executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings correspond to Markdown code fences, diagrams, framework examples, or words such as execute. No shell invocation, subprocess call, eval call, or backtick execution was found in executable skill code. Verdict: false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found illustrative Markdown and Python examples, not Ruby backticks or shell command execution. The executable script does not import subprocess or call shell APIs.

low_findings.title: External Links Are Reference Documentation
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded links to public documentation and a research paper. No code sends requests to those URLs, and no data collection or exfiltration behavior was found. Verdict: false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in an External resources list. No HTTP client usage or outbound request code is present in the reviewed executable script.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography And Key File Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The high-severity cryptography and key-file alerts do not map to cryptographic operations, certificates, private keys, or secret handling. The cited code uses terms like message_id, agent keys, dictionary keys, and winner selection. Verdict: false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts contain coordination terminology and dictionary operations, not cryptographic algorithms or credential material. No evidence of certificate or key file access was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Path Construction In Documentation Snippets
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference examples concatenate workspace paths with keys, resources, and workflow identifiers. This pattern can become path traversal if copied into production without validation, although it is not active runtime behavior in this skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and writes are visible in the examples. The security impact depends on whether future implementations accept untrusted identifiers.

content.value_statement: Complex AI tasks can exceed one agent context or require parallel expertise. This skill helps design coordinated agent systems with clear handoffs, consensus rules, and failure controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical multi-agent architecture patterns.
Helps choose coordination models based on context isolation and workflow control needs.
Provides handoff, direct forwarding, consensus, debate, and circuit breaker design guidance.
Includes Python examples for in-memory agent messaging, task assignment, handoffs, and voting.
Compares framework approaches for LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI style systems.
limitations: Does not provide a complete production agent runtime.
Example snippets require adaptation before handling untrusted inputs or persistent state.
Does not validate third-party framework versions or current API compatibility.
Multi-agent designs can increase token cost, latency, and operational complexity.

use_cases.title: Plan a research agent team
use_cases.description: Design a supervisor that delegates search, analysis, fact checking, and writing tasks while preserving source detail.

use_cases.title: Choose an orchestration pattern
use_cases.description: Compare centralized, swarm, and hierarchical patterns for a product workflow with different coordination needs.

use_cases.title: Review multi-agent failure modes
use_cases.description: Identify bottlenecks, handoff risks, consensus failures, and retry controls before deploying an agent workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a multi-agent architecture for this workflow: [describe workflow]. Compare supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design Agent Roles
prompt_templates.prompt: Design agent roles, handoff rules, and context boundaries for this task: [task]. Include inputs, outputs, and validation points.

prompt_templates.title: Add Consensus Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a consensus protocol for agents that may disagree about [decision]. Include weighting, debate rounds, and convergence checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Production Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this multi-agent design for bottlenecks, error propagation, infinite loops, and unsafe shared state: [architecture]. Recommend mitigations.

output_examples.input: Design a research workflow for market analysis.
output_examples.output: A supervisor delegates source gathering, data analysis, fact checking, and report writing. Handoffs include source lists, confidence notes, and validation checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Compare swarm and supervisor patterns for customer support routing.
output_examples.output: The supervisor pattern fits strict routing and audit needs. A swarm pattern fits flexible escalation, but needs stronger convergence and loop controls.

output_examples.input: Find risks in my multi-agent implementation plan.
output_examples.output: Key risks include supervisor context saturation, unclear handoff ownership, consensus bias, missing time limits, and shared-state conflicts.

best_practices: Use multi-agent designs when context isolation or parallel work provides clear value.
Define explicit handoff payloads, ownership, validation checks, and time limits.
Test failure modes such as bottlenecks, stale shared state, loops, and incorrect consensus.
anti_patterns: Creating agents only to mirror organization charts without technical need.
Letting agents pass full context when isolated summaries would be enough.
Using simple majority voting without confidence, expertise, or adversarial review.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps design multi-agent workflows, coordination protocols, handoffs, consensus mechanisms, and failure handling strategies.

faq.question: Does it run agents for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but it is not a hosted orchestration runtime.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: When should I avoid multi-agent architecture?
faq.answer: Avoid it when a single agent can solve the task with lower cost, lower latency, and simpler state management.

faq.question: Are the code examples production ready?
faq.answer: No. They are instructional examples and need input validation, persistence controls, observability, and security review.

faq.question: Does it cover specific frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes. It references LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI style implementation patterns.
